title: Case Study AAR: The Diving Wing Archetype

rationale.item: In Viperball, where drop kicks are a primary scoring weapon (4-6 per team per game), the ability to block a kick isn't just a special teams curiosity — it's a defensive philosophy. The question was: how do you model a kick-block specialist in a sport where kicking is woven into the offensive identity?

V1 set the bar too high: across 187 teams, roughly zero players qualified from pre-generated rosters. Blocked drop kicks happened at 2.5% (the base rate), entirely uninfluenced by personnel. The Diving Wing was a ghost archetype.

delegation_events.decision: Reframe the archetype from rare unicorn to a scaled, accessible specialist indexed on attributes
delegation_events.quote: The user's observation was simple and correct: "I think every level has their own version of a shutdown corner. It should just be indexed higher based on meeting attribute criteria."

actions.description: Lowered Diving Wing qualification thresholds: Tackling ≥ 72 (was 82), Speed ≥ 80 (was 88), Kicking awareness ≥ 45 (was 60).
Moved Diving Wing check from first to fourth priority in the flanker archetype cascade (Speed Flanker, Power Flanker, Elusive Flanker, Diving Wing, Reliable Flanker).

Replaced flat dk_block_bonus (+0.8%) with scaled attribute index: attr_index = tck_score × 0.45 + spd_score × 0.35 + kick_score × 0.20; block_bonus = 0.004 + attr_index × 0.008, ranging +0.4% to +1.2%.

risk_posture: The overall block rate barely moved (2.5% → 2.6%) because the DW bonus is intentionally modest — this is a rare, high-impact play.

validation_claims.claim: Measured Results (30-game batch): Teams with a Diving Wing ~0% → 72%; Avg DW per team ~0 → 2.4; tier distribution 6% Low, 90% Mid, 4% High; overall DK block rate 2.5% (base only) → 2.6% (base + DW bonus).
validation_claims.check_method: Re-run a 30-game batch and tabulate Diving Wing assignment rates and DK block rates.

section_headers_original: Case Study AAR: The Diving Wing Archetype
The Insight: Every Team Has a Shutdown Corner
1. Thresholds Create Cliffs, Indices Create Gradients
3. Small Bonuses, Large Population = Emergent Flavor
unmapped_content:
V1 details: thresholds Tackling ≥ 82, Speed ≥ 88, Kicking awareness ≥ 60; flat block bonus +0.8% on top of the 2.5% base rate. A flanker with 81 tackling and 87 speed — one tick below each threshold — contributed nothing to the block game.

Lowered Floor context: Average flanker attributes across the league sit at tck 72, spd 77, kick 71 — meaning the threshold is right at the population median for tackling, slightly above for speed, and well below for kicking.

Scaled bonus formula details: tck_score = (tackling - 70) / 30 (weight 45%); spd_score = (speed - 78) / 22 (weight 35%); kick_score = (kicking - 40) / 50 (weight 20%). Tier table: Low 0.0–0.3 → +0.55%, ~3.1% effective rate, "Scrappy, gets a hand up sometimes"; Mid 0.3–0.6 → +0.64–0.88%, ~3.2–3.4%, "Solid contributor, times it well"; High 0.6–0.85 → +0.88–1.08%, ~3.4–3.6%, "Legitimate kick-block threat"; Elite 0.85–1.0 → +1.08–1.2%, ~3.6–3.7%, "Shutdown specialist, alters kicking gameplan".

Design Lessons: 1. Thresholds Create Cliffs, Indices Create Gradients — the V2 approach creates a gradient where every point of tackling, speed, and kicking awareness contributes proportionally; there's no magic number where a player suddenly becomes a kick-block specialist. 2. Archetype Priority Protects Offensive Identity — a 95-speed flanker is a Speed Flanker who happens to be fast enough to block kicks; DW captures the "tweeners". 3. Small Bonuses, Large Population = Emergent Flavor — a +0.6% block bonus on one player doesn't change the game, but when 2-3 players per team carry that bonus it creates emergent matchup flavor and narrative pressure. 4. "Every Level Has Their Version" — argues against unicorn archetypes that only appear on elite rosters; keeps the mechanic relevant across the full 187-team league rather than being a feature only 5-10 teams ever see.
parser_notes: No date anywhere in the document or filename — recorded as undated. No explicit scope statement; case-study narrative format.
